splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether they may be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current information in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects revocation proceedings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee is a fugitive, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to extend the term beyond what Congress prescribed.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of Section 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits relitigating claims previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under Section 2255. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are subject to a stricter gatekeeping requirement than 2255(h) alone would impose.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements (newly discovered evidence or new rule of constitutional law) and are not subject to § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures such as mail-in ballot receipt deadlines. The resolution determines whether such challenges can proceed before elections or must wait until post-election disputes, affecting election administration nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when challenging state election laws that allegedly dilute lawfully cast votes or alter election outcomes.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates' alleged injuries from state election rules are too speculative or generalized to confer Article III standing for pre-election challenges.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or similar federal civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The circuits have applied differing standards for evaluating discrimination claims against hospitality and business operators, particularly regarding proof requirements and available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the split, suggesting the Second Circuit's approach diverges from other circuits.

positions.label: Second Circuit's approach
positions.summary: Applied legal standards to discrimination claims in the hospitality context that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled or divergent from other circuits to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in business establishments, creating the split that prompted Supreme Court review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to an agency's or the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of ambiguous provisions that carry criminal consequences. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails, while others have deferred to anti-defendant agency readings. Loper Bright's overruling of Chevron casts further doubt on the deference-first approach.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity must be applied before deferring to an agency or Sentencing Commission interpretation of an ambiguous criminal or sentencing provision, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive guidance.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that courts should defer to an agency's or the Sentencing Commission's reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous provision, even in the criminal sentencing context, before resorting to the rule of lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (requiring only one).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) or if possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This split directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses and creates disparate outcomes depending on geography.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they satisfy all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference after Kisor v. Wilkie.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary adding conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' deserves controlling deference under Kisor/Stinson, or whether the guideline text unambiguously excludes such offenses. This affects career-offender designations and substantially longer sentences for many defendants.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 reasonably interprets a genuinely ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight, so inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text allows, so deference is inappropriate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the career-offender and other sentencing guideline provisions defining 'controlled substance offense' require the underlying substance to be federally scheduled. Some circuits apply a broad reading encompassing state-law-only substances, while others require a federal-law nexus. This affects guideline calculations for many defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: No federal-scheduling requirement
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines broadly includes state drug offenses regardless of whether the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Federal-scheduling requirement applies
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under federal law, applying a categorical or modified categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory stacked § 924(c) sentences — can be considered as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on such changes, while others permit courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to resolve this but has generated further disagreement about the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot, alone or in combination with other factors, constitute an extraordinary and compelling reason for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others in determining whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the terms of imprisonment exclusively through habeas corpus, or whether they may also use civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens or the APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging conditions or terms of confinement that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of imprisonment, or whether civil rights and other statutory actions are also available. This affects prisoners challenging BOP decisions on matters like earned time credits under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the execution of a sentence, including conditions and terms of confinement, must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241.

positions.label: Civil rights actions are also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may challenge conditions of confinement through civil rights actions in addition to habeas, particularly when the challenge does not seek immediate or speedier release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for violations of supervised release when imposing a revocation sentence, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit permits courts to expressly punish defendants for their violation conduct, while other circuits limit revocation sentences to addressing the breach of the court's trust inherent in the supervisory relationship. This affects sentencing for hundreds of supervised release revocations annually.

positions.label: Punishment for violation conduct is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences that expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying their supervised release violations.

positions.label: Revocation sentences address breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should primarily address the defendant's breach of the court's trust, not serve as additional punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to appeals from the denial of attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil notice of appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises from a criminal case, the 14-day criminal notice of appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between a Chapter 13 bankruptcy filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A). The Ninth Circuit held such appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that protect debtors' interest in post-petition appreciation to encourage good-faith Chapter 13 filings.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a), post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects good-faith converting debtors by limiting the estate to the value of property as of the original petition date, so post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order.' This potentially creates tension with circuits that have taken a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine's scope regarding privacy interests in litigation documents.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records qualifies for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost if review is delayed.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because the privacy interest can be adequately vindicated on appeal from a final judgment or through other means.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit it, while others permit it either alone or in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) allowing it has further complicated the split.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot contribute
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the FAAAA's preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries caused by carriers they selected.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they are too tenuously connected to the broker's transportation services to fall within the FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label that the EPA did not require. The question affects thousands of product liability cases involving agricultural chemicals.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims when they are based on duties that parallel rather than add to FIFRA's requirements, consistent with Bates v. Dow Agrosciences.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas-type challenges — such as arguments about errors in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit courts to consider such claims while others hold compassionate release cannot serve as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence, including claims that could also be raised in habeas, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis.

positions.label: May not consider habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release is not a substitute for habeas corpus and courts may not rely on challenges to the validity of a conviction or sentence as extraordinary and compelling reasons for release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
